sections.heading: Prognosis
sections.content: The prognosis for cutaneous lymphomas varies significantly by disease subtype, stage, and histologic features. For primary cutaneous B-cell lymphomas (PC-BCL), primary cutaneous follicle center lymphoma (PCFCL) and primary cutaneous marginal zone lymphoma (PCMZL) are indolent, with 5-year overall survival (OS) rates exceeding 95% and 99%, respectively [1,2]. In contrast, primary cutaneous diffuse large B-cell lymphoma, leg type (PC-DLBCL, leg type) is aggressive, with a 5-year OS rate of approximately 50% [2,3]. For mycosis fungoides (MF) and Sézary syndrome (SS), prognosis is highly dependent on clinical stage: patients with early-stage disease (IA-IB) have an excellent prognosis with 5-year OS rates >90%, while advanced-stage disease (IIB-IV) carries a poorer prognosis, with 5-year OS rates ranging from 28% to 68% based on prognostic models [4]. Prognostic factors include age, T classification, presence of extracutaneous disease, extent of blood involvement, elevated lactate dehydrogenase (LDH), and large cell transformation (LCT) [4,5]. LCT is an independent adverse prognostic factor; in one analysis of 117 patients with SS, median OS was 35 months for those with LCT versus 80 months without LCT [6]. For primary cutaneous CD30+ T-cell lymphoproliferative disorders, primary cutaneous anaplastic large cell lymphoma (PC-ALCL) has a favorable prognosis with 5-year OS rates of 81%–91%, while lymphomatoid papulosis (LyP) is benign with a 5-year OS rate of 92% [7,8]. Subcutaneous panniculitis-like T-cell lymphoma (SPTCL) has a 5-year OS rate of 80%, but this drops to approximately 46% when complicated by hemophagocytic lymphohistiocytosis (HLH) [9]. Primary cutaneous indolent T-cell lymphoproliferative disorders (PCSM-TLPD and PCA-TLPD) have an excellent prognosis with 5-year OS rates of 100% [10].

factors.factor: Age, T classification, extracutaneous disease, blood involvement, elevated LDH, LCT (multivariate prognostic factors)

Subcutaneous panniculitis-like T-cell lymphoma (SPTCL) has a 5-year OS rate of 80%, but this drops to approximately 46% when complicated by hemophagocytic lymphohistiocytosis (HLH) [9].
